items.title: PHISH ALERT: Press Play for Compromise — Voicemail Phishing Kit Bundles SSO Hijacking, Credential Theft, and RMM Delivery
items.description: An advanced voicemail-themed phishing campaign is utilizing HTML attachments to hijack Microsoft 365 sessions through silent OAuth exploitation. Emails arrive spoofing legitimate businesses with fake voicemail notifications containing embedded HTML files. When victims click the play button, the kit triggers a rogue OAuth 2.0 request using the prompt=none parameter to steal authentication tokens fr
items.ai_summary: The PHISH ALERT campaign utilizes a sophisticated voicemail-themed phishing kit to hijack Microsoft 365 sessions through silent OAuth exploitation, specifically targeting users with fake voicemail notifications containing embedded HTML files. When victims click the play button, the kit triggers a rogue OAuth 2.0 request using the prompt=none parameter to steal authentication tokens, allowing attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information. This campaign has real-world impact as it can lead to credential theft, SSO hijacking, and RMM delivery, affecting various organizations and individuals who use Microsoft 365. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific security measures such as configuring Microsoft 365 to use conditional access policies, enabling multi-factor authentication, and educating users about the risks of phishing attacks. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and update their security software to the latest versions, such as Microsoft 365 version 22.02 or later, to mitigate the risk of exploitation.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1212| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Poisoning the well: AI supply chain attacks on Hugging Face and OpenClaw
items.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and ClawHub to deliver malware by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions. Over 575 malicious skills across 13 developer accounts were identified in the OpenClaw ecosystem, targeting Windows and macOS with trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer. Attackers abuse trust relationships betw
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and OpenClaw to deliver malware by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions, with over 575 malicious skills identified across 13 developer accounts in the OpenClaw ecosystem. The attackers are targeting Windows and macOS systems with trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer, leveraging trust relationships between developers and users. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict validation and verification of AI models and datasets before deployment, and ensure that all dependencies and extensions are from trusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing AI-powered applications and extensions, and monitor their systems for suspicious activity. Developers should also prioritize secure coding practices and regularly audit their codebases for potential vulnerabilities.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ransomware Analysis: Go Binary and Fast Encryption
items.description: The Gentlemen is a Ransomware-as-a-Service operation, tracked as Storm-2697, that emerged in mid-2025 after splitting from Qilin ransomware following a payment dispute. Operating as a highly structured syndicate with at least 9 core operators, the group has compromised over 1,570 organizations across 70+ countries, with approximately 71-78% paying ransoms and never appearing on public leak sites.
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware, tracked as Storm-2697, is a highly structured Ransomware-as-a-Service operation that emerged in mid-2025. This threat has compromised over 1,570 organizations across 70+ countries, with approximately 71-78% of victims paying ransoms. The attack vector involves obtaining capabilities through T1588, allowing the threat actors to encrypt data for impact using T1486, resulting in significant financial losses for affected organizations. To remediate this threat, defenders should focus on implementing robust backup and recovery procedures, ensuring that all sensitive data is encrypted, and conducting regular security audits to identify potential vulnerabilities. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing a ransomware-specific incident response plan, which includes procedures for responding to and containing ransomware attacks, such as isolating infected systems and restoring data from backups.

graph LR

    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1070| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1482| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: SilabRAT, What's Your Power?
items.description: SilabRAT is an advanced Remote Access Trojan offered as Malware-as-a-Service on Darkweb forums since late 2025, developed by threat actor o1oo1 and sold for $5,000 monthly. This financially-motivated tool focuses on credential theft and cryptocurrency operations, featuring Hidden Virtual Network Computing for invisible remote control, browser profile cloning to bypass session protections, and auto
items.ai_summary: The SilabRAT Remote Access Trojan, developed by threat actor o1oo1, is a highly advanced malware tool offered as Malware-as-a-Service on Darkweb forums since late 2025. This financially-motivated tool is sold for $5,000 monthly and focuses on credential theft and cryptocurrency operations. The attack vector involves the use of Hidden Virtual Network Computing for invisible remote control and browser profile cloning to bypass session protections. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities, specifically focusing on unusual VNC and remote access software activity, and ensure that all remote access tools are properly secured and up-to-date. Additionally, defenders should prioritize credential theft protection by enforcing strong password policies, multi-factor authentication, and regular password rotations.

items.title: Fake Software Tutorials on TikTok Spread Vidar Stealer
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging TikTok and Instagram Reels to distribute the Vidar infostealer through fake software tutorials. Two distinct campaigns use short-form videos disguised as tutorials for unlocking premium software like Spotify. The first campaign uses accounts mimicking official Windows profiles with AI-voiced clips instructing users to run PowerShell commands that download Vidar from lo
items.ai_summary: The Vidar infostealer is being distributed through fake software tutorials on TikTok and Instagram Reels, with threat actors leveraging short-form videos to instruct users to run PowerShell commands that download the malware. The campaigns use accounts mimicking official Windows profiles, with AI-voiced clips providing instructions. If exploited, users may have their sensitive information stolen, including login credentials and financial data. To remediate, users should avoid running PowerShell commands from untrusted sources and ensure their systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, users can enable PowerShell logging and monitoring to detect suspicious activity, and consider implementing a security orchestration, automation, and response (SOAR) solution to streamline incident response.

items.title: China-Linked Fake Consulting Sites Targeting US Clearance Holders Seized
items.description: US Federal authorities have seized 13 domains allegedly used in a Chinese intelligence-linked operation to recruit Americans with access to classified government information. The websites posed as legitimate consulting firms, offering vague consultancy and advisory roles to current and former US government employees, military personnel, and security clearance holders. The operation, which began in
items.ai_summary: The threat involves China-linked fake consulting sites targeting US clearance holders, with 13 domains seized by US Federal authorities. The attack vector involves social engineering, where the websites pose as legitimate consulting firms, offering vague consultancy and advisory roles to current and former US government employees, military personnel, and security clearance holders. The real-world impact of this threat is the potential compromise of classified government information, which could have significant national security implications. To remediate this threat, defenders should be cautious of unsolicited job offers or consulting opportunities, especially those that seem vague or require access to sensitive information. Additionally, individuals with security clearances should verify the legitimacy of any consulting firm or organization before engaging with them, and report any suspicious activity to the relevant authorities.

items.title: Vibe Hacking: Two AI-Augmented Campaigns Target Government and Financial Sectors in Latin America
items.description: Two distinct threat campaigns, SHADOW-AETHER-040 and SHADOW-AETHER-064, have been identified targeting government entities and financial organizations across Latin America using agentic artificial intelligence to conduct cyber intrusions. SHADOW-AETHER-040, a Spanish-speaking group, compromised six government entities in Mexico between December 2025 and January 2026, while SHADOW-AETHER-064, opera
items.ai_summary: The SHADOW-AETHER-040 and SHADOW-AETHER-064 campaigns, targeting government and financial sectors in Latin America, utilize AI-augmented techniques for cyber intrusions. The attacks, which occurred between December 2025 and January 2026, compromised six government entities in Mexico, with the Spanish-speaking group SHADOW-AETHER-040 being the primary actor. The real-world impact of these attacks includes potential data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information, affecting government entities and financial organizations. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement robust security measures, including multi-factor authentication, regular security audits, and employee training on AI-driven social engineering tactics. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or data transfers, and update their threat intelligence feeds to include information on these campaigns.

items.title: Sniper's Nest: From Brand Impersonation to Browser Hijacking and CPA Fraud
items.description: An investigation into phishing activity targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa uncovered SniperDz, a centralized Push-Notification-as-a-Service and Phishing-as-a-Service platform. The operation uses fraudulent Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, and trusted organizations to promote fake offers including free mobile internet packages and financial compensat
items.ai_summary: The Sniper's Nest operation utilizes phishing tactics, specifically T1566, to target users in the Middle East and North Africa, impersonating trusted figures and organizations to promote fake offers. The attack vector involves fraudulent Facebook accounts and Push-Notification-as-a-Service, leading to browser hijacking and CPA fraud. If exploited, users may experience financial loss and compromised personal data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email and social media filters, and educate users on identifying phishing attempts, particularly those using brand impersonation. Additionally, users should be cautious of suspicious links and offers, and report any fraudulent activity to the relevant authorities.

items.title: From external espionage to domestic targeting
items.description: Analysis of OceanLotus activities from 2024-2026 reveals a strategic shift toward domestic espionage within Vietnam. The Vietnam-aligned APT group conducted two distinct campaigns using the SPECTRALVIPER backdoor: a supply-chain attack compromising FireAnt Metakit stock trading platform from October 2025 to March 2026, and a prolonged intrusion into a Vietnamese infrastructure and transport constr
items.ai_summary: The OceanLotus APT group has shifted its focus towards domestic espionage within Vietnam, conducting two distinct campaigns using the SPECTRALVIPER backdoor. The first campaign involved a supply-chain attack on the FireAnt Metakit stock trading platform from October 2025 to March 2026, compromising the platform and potentially allowing the group to gain access to sensitive financial information. The second campaign involved a prolonged intrusion into a Vietnamese infrastructure and transport construction company, highlighting the group's ability to adapt and expand its targeting. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities, focusing on unusual patterns of remote access software usage, and ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, organizations should conduct regular security audits and risk assessments to identify potential vulnerabilities and weaknesses in their systems and networks.

items.title: Targeted espionage against Cambodian government entities
items.description: Acronis Threat Research Unit identified two espionage campaigns targeting Cambodian government entities in defense and public works sectors, attributed to a cluster tracked as Khmer Shadow. Both campaigns delivered a custom C++ loader named NIGHTFORGE through government-themed lures in self-extracting archives. NIGHTFORGE employs sophisticated evasion techniques including NTDLL unhooking and Hell'
items.ai_summary: The NIGHTFORGE custom C++ loader is delivered through self-extracting archives with government-themed lures, targeting Cambodian government entities in the defense and public works sectors. This loader employs sophisticated evasion techniques, including NTDLL unhooking and Hell's Gate evasion, to avoid detection. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for data encryption and exfiltration, as well as impairment of defenses, which could lead to significant disruptions in government operations. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and user education to prevent the initial execution of the malicious archives, and ensure that all systems are configured to detect and block suspicious API calls, such as those associated with T1106. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation and access controls to limit the potential impact of a successful breach.

items.title: CVE-2026-10283: A vulnerability was detected in Bottelet DaybydayCRM up to 2.2.1. Affected is an...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in Bottelet DaybydayCRM up to 2.2.1. Affected is an unknown function of the component Setting Handler. Performing a manipulation results in missing authentication. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. It is recommended to apply a patch to fix this issue.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10283 is a vulnerability in Bottelet DaybydayCRM versions up to 2.2.1, where an unknown function of the Setting Handler component is affected, allowing for missing authentication. This can be exploited remotely, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data. The real-world impact of this vulnerability could result in data breaches or unauthorized modifications to CRM data, affecting businesses that rely on DaybydayCRM for customer relationship management. To remediate this issue, defenders should apply the patch for DaybydayCRM version 2.2.2 or later, and ensure that all instances of the software are updated to prevent exploitation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement additional security measures, such as multi-factor authentication, to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: CVE-2026-10282: A security vulnerability has been detected in Bottelet DaybydayCRM up to 2.2.1. ...
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in Bottelet DaybydayCRM up to 2.2.1. This impacts the function view of the file app/Http/Controllers/DocumentsController.php. Such manipulation leads to improper authorization. The attack may be launched remotely. It is best practice to apply a patch to resolve this issue.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10282 is a security vulnerability in Bottelet DaybydayCRM versions up to 2.2.1, specifically affecting the DocumentsController.php file in the app/Http/Controllers directory. This vulnerability allows for improper authorization, which can be exploited remotely. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, with potential real-world impacts including data breaches and lateral movement within a network. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should apply the patch to update DaybydayCRM to a version later than 2.2.1, and ensure that all related dependencies and configurations are updated accordingly. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious RDP activity, as TTPs such as T1563.002 RDP Hijacking may be used in conjunction with this vulnerability.

items.description: A weakness has been identified in Enderfga claw-orchestrator up to 3.5.5. This affects the function EmbeddedServer of the file src/embedded-server.ts of the component API Endpoint. This manipulation causes missing authentication. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. Upgrading to version 3.5.6 mitigates this issue. Pa
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10281 is a weakness in Enderfga claw-orchestrator up to version 3.5.5, specifically affecting the EmbeddedServer function in the src/embedded-server.ts file of the API Endpoint component. This vulnerability allows for missing authentication, which can be exploited remotely. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to the system, potentially affecting all users of the Enderfga claw-orchestrator. To remediate this issue, defenders should upgrade to version 3.5.6, ensuring that the EmbeddedServer function is properly authenticated. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for any suspicious activity and consider implementing additional security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits.

items.title: CVE-2026-10280: A security flaw has been discovered in horizon921 mcpilot 0.1.0. The impacted el...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in horizon921 mcpilot 0.1.0. The impacted element is an unknown function of the file client/src/app/api/mcp/call/route.ts of the component MCP API Call Endpoint. The manipulation of the argument serverBaseUrl results in server-side request forgery. The attack can be launched remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The p
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10280 is a security flaw in horizon921 mcpilot version 0.1.0, specifically affecting the MCP API Call Endpoint in the file client/src/app/api/mcp/call/route.ts. The vulnerability allows for server-side request forgery through the manipulation of the serverBaseUrl argument, which can be exploited remotely. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to internal resources, potentially affecting all users of the impacted system. To remediate, defenders should update to a patched version of horizon921 mcpilot, if available, and ensure that all API calls are validated and sanitized to prevent malicious input. Additionally, implementing a web application firewall (WAF) can help detect and prevent such attacks.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-10279 is a vulnerability in the hiraishikentaro wezterm-mcp 0.1.0 package, specifically in the src/wezterm_executor.ts file, which allows for os command injection via the manipulation of the request.params.arguments.pane_id argument. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and control of affected systems, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to a patched version of wezterm-mcp, if available, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to os command injection and implement measures to prevent remote exploitation, such as restricting access to the affected component and validating user input.

items.title: CVE-2026-0072: In addInputMethodListener of com.android.server.inputmethod.InputMethodManagerSe...
items.description: In addInputMethodListener of com.android.server.inputmethod.InputMethodManagerService, there is a missing permission check. This could lead to local escalation of privilege with no additional execution privileges needed. User interaction is not needed for exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2024-52011: launch-editor allows users to open files with line numbers in editor from Node.j...
items.description: launch-editor allows users to open files with line numbers in editor from Node.js. Prior to version 2.9.0, due to the insufficient sanitization of the `file` argument in the `launchEditor`, an attacker can execute arbitrary commands on Windows by supplying a filename that contains special characters. This issue has been fixed in the `launch-editor` version 2.9.0, corresponding to vite version 5.4.

items.title: Cyber Force not included in Senate defense policy roadmap
items.description: An amendment by Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand (D-NY) to the chamber’s fiscal 2027 national defense authorization bill that would have created the digital-focused service was defeated 14-13 when the Senate Armed Services Committee took up the nearly $1.2 trillion legislation behind closed doors this week.

items.title: Authorities dismantle 'AudiA6' ransomware crypto-laundering service
items.description: Law enforcement has dismantled the “AudiA6” cryptocurrency service allegedly used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million. [...]

items.title: British high school sends students home following cyberattack
items.description: Great Marlow School, which has 1,428 pupils according to the Department for Education (DfE), said it was set to remain closed while it works with specialist IT and cybersecurity professionals to resolve the issue.

items.title: Hacker linked to Void Blizzard faces charges over cyberespionage campaign
items.description: Denis Obrezko, 36, made his initial appearance in federal court in Boston on Tuesday after being transferred to U.S. custody from Thailand, where he was arrested last November.

items.title: Segmentation Works for OT If Operators Are Paying Attention
items.description: Operational technology security remains as difficult as ever, with even the best practice recommendation falling short.

items.title: Why AI-driven threats are exposing the limits of MSP security stacks
items.description: AI-driven attacks are exposing the limits of fragmented MSP security stacks and slow response workflows. Kaseya breaks down why integrated security, automation, and recovery are becoming essential. [...]

items.title: Oracle Addresses PeopleSoft Vulnerability Amid Reports of Zero-Day Attacks
items.description: Oracle has released mitigations for CVE-2026-35273, but it has not said whether it’s a zero-day exploited in ShinyHunters attacks. The post Oracle Addresses PeopleSoft Vulnerability Amid Reports of Zero-Day Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: University of Nottingham confirms cyber incident as Shiny Hunters group claims data theft
items.description: According to the university’s statement, it is still working to understand what data has been accessed and said it had already directly contacted affected students and alumni, potentially including those in its foreign campuses in Malaysia and China as well as in Nottingham.

items.title: Alert Fatigue Is Becoming a Security Threat of Its Own
items.description: As alert volumes outpace human capacity, organizations are turning to AI, automation, and deeper context to separate real threats from the noise. The post Alert Fatigue Is Becoming a Security Threat of Its Own appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybersecurity Stars Awards 2026: Winners Announced Across 95 Categories
items.description: Most good security work is invisible by design. Today is the exception. The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners are announced across 95 subcategories in four main award categories. The reason is simple. Cybersecurity is full of work that deserves recognition and rarely gets it. Products that quietly close real gaps. Teams that stop incidents nobody reads about. Companies that raise the

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Worm Code Leaked, AI Agent Phished, Claude Code Patch + 28 New Stories
items.description: It's been one of those weeks. You expect the usual noise: recycled malware, sloppy attacks, another easy target getting hit. Instead, there's a supply chain attack kit in a public repo, a $5,000-a-month RAT that clones browsers, and research showing AI agents can be tricked into leaking real credentials. The bigger problem is how polished this all looks now. Mule networks run like SaaS.

items.title: CISA Directs Federal Agencies to Prioritize Security Patches Based on Risk
items.description: The new BOD 26-04 requires agencies to review and update vulnerability management policies with a focus on KEV catalog entries. The post CISA Directs Federal Agencies to Prioritize Security Patches Based on Risk appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: OnyxC2 Stealer Offers Cybercriminals Enterprise-Grade Theft for $250 a Month
items.description: Researchers say the OnyxC2 malware targets more than 200 applications and extensions while evading detection through encrypted payloads, DLL sideloading, and in-memory execution techniques. The post OnyxC2 Stealer Offers Cybercriminals Enterprise-Grade Theft for $250 a Month appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Coupang hit with record $409 million data breach fine in Korea
items.description: ​​The Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC), South Korea's data protection regulator, has fined e-commerce giant Coupang a record 624.6 billion won (roughly $409 million) following a massive data breach affecting more than 37 million customers [...]

items.title: CISA tells govt agencies to patch critical exploited flaws in 3 days
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) announced a new Binding Operational Directive, 26-04, that prioritizes security updates for Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies. [...]

items.title: Hackers Exploit Langflow Vulnerability for Remote Code Execution
items.description: Disclosed in March, the security defect enables unauthenticated attackers to write files to arbitrary locations on the system. The post Hackers Exploit Langflow Vulnerability for Remote Code Execution appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Siemens Says Desigo CC Files Flagged as Malware by Security Engines
items.description: A PowerShell script included in patch files appears to be triggering false positives by multiple security engines. The post Siemens Says Desigo CC Files Flagged as Malware by Security Engines appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS.
items.description: For thirty years, vulnerability management ran on a buffer: the months between when a vulnerability was found and when someone could figure out how to weaponize it. The solution was straightforward enough; triage by severity, schedule the fix, validate, and move on. The buffer was what made that work. Today, that buffer is gone. AI didn't make your team slower. It changed the other side of the

items.title: FBI Seizes 13 Websites That Officials Say Were Used by China to Target and Recruit US Workers
items.description: The 13 websites purported to be affiliated with consulting companies that advertised job openings for current and former holders of security clearances The post FBI Seizes 13 Websites That Officials Say Were Used by China to Target and Recruit US Workers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Splunk, Palo Alto Networks Patch Severe Vulnerabilities
items.description: The security defects could allow attackers to create or modify arbitrary files and access and modify protected resources. The post Splunk, Palo Alto Networks Patch Severe Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ‘GreatXML’ Zero-Day Exploit Bypasses BitLocker
items.description: The PoC exploits Microsoft Defender’s offline scan to spawn a SYSTEM shell when rebooting in Recovery Mode. The post ‘GreatXML’ Zero-Day Exploit Bypasses BitLocker appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: OceanLotus Hits Vietnam Investors With SPECTRALVIPER in FireAnt Attack
items.description: The Vietnam-aligned threat actor known as OceanLotus has been attributed to two distinct campaigns that targeted domestic entities and stock investors with a backdoor known as SPECTRALVIPER. The campaigns involve a prolonged cyber espionage operation aimed at a Vietnamese infrastructure and transport construction corporation between mid-2024 and February 2026, as well as a supply chain attack

items.title: Microsoft fixes BitLocker recovery bug on Windows Server 2025
items.description: Microsoft has resolved a known issue causing some Windows Server 2025 devices to boot into BitLocker recovery after installing the April 2026 security update. [...]

items.title: University of Nottingham Confirms Breach After Hackers Leak Data
items.description: The ShinyHunters hacker group has taken credit for the attack, leaking more than 450,000 email addresses and other information. The post University of Nottingham Confirms Breach After Hackers Leak Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Nottingham University data breach affects over 450,000 students
items.description: The University of Nottingham confirmed on Wednesday that a hacking group gained access to its student records system in a breach affecting both current students and alums. [...]

items.title: GitHub to Disable npm Install Scripts by Default to Stop Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: GitHub has announced what it said are "breaking changes" coming to npm version 12, one of which turns off install scripts by default to combat software supply chain threats. The changes aim to combat attack techniques that abuse the "npm install" command to trigger the execution of malicious code using npm lifecycle hooks. "Npm install" is used to download and install all the necessary

items.title: Max severity Ivanti Sentry vulnerability now exploited in attacks
items.description: Attackers are now targeting a recently patched maximum-severity flaw in Ivanti Sentry, enabling them to execute code with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. [...]

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9968, (Thu, Jun 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Chinese, N. Korean Threat Groups Build on Asia-Pacific Success
items.description: North Korea's gross domestic product (GDP) has grown, in part because of the nation's state-sponsored cybercrime groups, which target financial firms and other businesses.

items.title: Path traversal flaw in AI dev platform Langflow exploited in attacks
items.description: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal vulnerability in the AI development platform Langflow, to write arbitrary files on exposed servers. [...]

items.title: CISA Rewrites Federal Patching Requirements for AI Threat Era
items.description: The new directive gives federal agencies three days to fix the most dangerous flaws, while less severe issues can be deferred.

items.title: The ‘Miasma’ worm source code briefly leaked on GitHub
items.description: The Miasma credential-stealing attack framework, which has recently targeted open-source ecosystems through supply-chain attacks, was briefly open-sourced on GitHub. [...]

items.title: Bug Bounty Research Triggers ServiceNow Security Alert
items.description: Security research inadvertently led organizations to believe they were being breached through their ServiceNow instances.

items.title: CISA to require federal agencies to patch some cyber vulnerabilities within 3 days
items.description: CISA is giving agencies 180 days to adopt the new patching time frame, according to a directive released Wednesday.

items.title: GitHub announces npm security changes to tackle supply-chain attacks
items.description: GitHub has announced that npm v12, expected next month, will introduce several security-focused changes aimed at blocking supply-chain attacks abusing behaviors triggered by the 'npm install' command. [...]

items.title: AI Risk Worries Insurers & Businesses Alike
items.description: As companies adopt AI, many insurance firms are explicitly excluding AI risks, while others are forging ahead to create the right framework. What risks can firms reasonably manage?

items.title: Nightmare-Eclipse Drops Yet Another Microsoft Exploit, RoguePlanet
items.description: The disgruntled researcher released yet another PoC for a Windows Defender bug that allows for system takeover, showing no signs of abandoning their ongoing feud with Microsoft.

items.title: China-Linked JDY Botnet Expands to 1,500+ Devices for Cyber Reconnaissance
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have warned of a "resurgence and expansion" of JDY, a covert network associated with China-nexus state-sponsored threat actors. "The JDY botnet comprises over 1,500 SOHO [small office and home office] and IoT devices and operates as a centrally controlled, high-performance scanner used to discover, fingerprint, and continuously map exposed services at scale," Lumen's

items.title: Ivanti, Fortinet, and SAP Release Patches for Multiple Critical Vulnerabilities
items.description: Fortinet, Ivanti, and SAP have released security updates to address multiple critical security vulnerabilities that could result in arbitrary code execution and information disclosure. The security flaw patched by Fortinet relates to a command injection vulnerability in FortiSandbox, FortiSandbox Cloud, and FortiSandbox PaaS WEB UI. It's tracked as CVE-2026-25089 (CVSS score: 9.1). "An

items.title: Unpatched Langflow Flaw CVE-2026-5027 Exploited for Unauthenticated RCE
items.description: A high-severity unpatched security flaw in Langflow, an open-source low-code platform to build artificial intelligence (AI) applications, has come under active exploitation in the wild, according to findings from VulnCheck. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-5027 (CVSS score: 8.8), a case of path traversal that could allow an attacker to write files to arbitrary locations. "The 'POST /

items.title: CISA Adds Cisco, Chrome, and Arista Flaws to KEV Catalog Amid Active Exploitation
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday added three new vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, following reports of active exploitation. The list of vulnerabilities is as follows - CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8) - An improper encoding or escaping of output vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that could allow an

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: The Invisible Battlefield: How Cyberwar Is Reshaping Everyday Life
items.description: Former National Cyber Director Chris Inglis warns that cyberattacks threaten hospitals, utilities, and essential services.

items.title: Blame AI: Patch Tuesday Hits Record 206 CVEs
items.description: Voluminous patch updates could soon be the norm, as artificial intelligence accelerates the speed and scale of vulnerability discovery.

items.title: Microsoft Exchange Flaw Lets Attackers Spoof Any Email Address
items.description: “Ghost-Sender" is the result of a widespread misconfiguration, according to researchers, and evidence indicates it's being actively abused in the wild.

items.title: Miasma Supply Chain Worm Burrows Into 73 Microsoft Repositories
items.description: The attacks stemmed from a GitHub account that was also compromised in a previous Miasma attack on Microsoft last month.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-06-07, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: This diary continues the Internet Storm Center&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s tracking of the TeamPCP supply chain campaign, first documented in the SANS white paper When the Security Scanner Became the Weapon and most recently in the handler diary Activity Through 2026-05-24. Since that update, the story moved into two new places: the United States government, which formally caught up to the campaign, and the wider population of attackers now wielding the Mini Shai-Hulud framework that TeamPCP open-sourced last month.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9962, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 5th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9960, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
